***Hawaii Five-O*** is an American [police procedural](/wiki/Police_procedural "Police procedural") [drama](/wiki/Drama "Drama") series produced by [CBS Productions](/wiki/CBS_Productions "CBS Productions") and created by [Leonard Freeman](/wiki/Leonard_Freeman "Leonard Freeman"). Set in [Hawaii](/wiki/Hawaii "Hawaii"), the show aired for 12 seasons on [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS") from September 20, 1968, to April 5, 1980. The show starred [Jack Lord](/wiki/Jack_Lord "Jack Lord") as Detective Captain [Stephen "Steve" McGarrett](/wiki/Steve_McGarrett "Steve McGarrett"), the head of a fictional state police task force in Hawaii. The [theme music](/wiki/Theme_music "Theme music") composed by [Morton Stevens](/wiki/Morton_Stevens "Morton Stevens") became especially popular. Many episodes in the series would end with McGarrett's [catchphrase](/wiki/Catchphrase "Catchphrase"), "Book 'em, Danno!"

At the airing of its finale, it was the longest-running police drama in American television history, and the last scripted primetime show that debuted in the 1960s to leave the air.[[a]](#cite_note-2)

## Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hawaii_Five-O_(1968_TV_series)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Overview")]

The [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS") television network [produced](/wiki/CBS_Productions "CBS Productions") *Hawaii Five-O*, which aired from September 20, 1968, to April 5, 1980. The program continues to be broadcast in [syndication](/wiki/Television_syndication "Television syndication") worldwide. Created by [Leonard Freeman](/wiki/Leonard_Freeman "Leonard Freeman"), *Hawaii Five-O* was shot on location in [Honolulu, Hawaiʻi](/wiki/Honolulu "Honolulu"), and throughout the island of [Oʻahu](/wiki/Oahu "Oahu") and elsewhere in the [Hawaiian Islands](/wiki/Hawaiian_Islands "Hawaiian Islands") with occasional filming in locales such as [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"), [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"), and [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong").

The show centers on an elite fictional [state police](/wiki/State_police "State police") unit led by former U.S. Naval officer Steve McGarrett (played by [Jack Lord](/wiki/Jack_Lord "Jack Lord")), a detective captain, appointed by the [Governor](/wiki/Governor_of_Hawaii "Governor of Hawaii"), Paul Jameson. In the show, McGarrett oversees state police officers – the young [Danny "Danno" Williams](/wiki/Danny_%22Danno%22_Williams "Danny \"Danno\" Williams"), veteran Chin Ho Kelly ([Kam Fong Chun](/wiki/Kam_Fong_Chun "Kam Fong Chun")), and streetwise Kono Kalakaua for seasons one through four. [Honolulu Police Department](/wiki/Honolulu_Police_Department "Honolulu Police Department") Officer Duke Lukela joined the team as a regular, as did Ben Kokua, who replaced Kono beginning with season five. Occasionally, McGarrett's Five-O team is assisted by other officers as needed: Det. Frank Kamana ([Douglas Mossman](/wiki/Douglas_Mossman "Douglas Mossman")), P.O. Sandi Wells ([Amanda McBroom](/wiki/Amanda_McBroom "Amanda McBroom")), medical examiner Doc Bergman (Al Eben), forensic specialist Che Fong ([Harry Endo](/wiki/Harry_Endo "Harry Endo")), and a secretary.[[2]](#cite_note-Terrace-3)

The title of the show refers to Hawaiʻi's status as the fiftieth U.S. state. At the time of the show's premiere, Hawaiʻi had been a state for nine years.[[3]](#cite_note-4) The *Five-O* team consists of three to five members (small for a real state police unit), and is depicted as occupying a suite of offices at the [ʻIolani Palace](/wiki/%CA%BBIolani_Palace "ʻIolani Palace").[[4]](#cite_note-Snauffer-5) Five-O lacks its own radio network, necessitating frequent requests by McGarrett to the Honolulu Police Department dispatchers.

For 12 seasons, McGarrett and his team pursued international secret agents, criminals, and [organized crime](/wiki/Organized_crime "Organized crime") syndicates plaguing the Hawaiian Islands. With the aid of [District Attorney](/wiki/District_Attorney "District Attorney") and later [Hawaiʻi's Attorney General](/wiki/Attorney_General_of_Hawaii "Attorney General of Hawaii") John Manicote, McGarrett is successful in sending most of his enemies to [prison](/wiki/Prison "Prison"). One such crime syndicate, introduced in the fifth season, was led by the Vashon family, portrayed by [Luther Adler](/wiki/Luther_Adler "Luther Adler") and [Harold Gould](/wiki/Harold_Gould "Harold Gould"). Other criminals and organized crime bosses on the islands were played by actors such as [Ross Martin](/wiki/Ross_Martin "Ross Martin"), [Gavin MacLeod](/wiki/Gavin_MacLeod "Gavin MacLeod"), and [Ricardo Montalban](/wiki/Ricardo_Montalban "Ricardo Montalban").

By the 12th and final season, series regular James MacArthur had left the show (in 1996, he admitted that he had become tired of the role and wanted to do other things), as had Kam Fong. Unlike other characters before him, Fong's character, Chin Ho Kelly, at Fong's request, was killed off, murdered while working undercover to expose a protection ring in Chinatown in the last episode of season 10. New characters Jim 'Kimo' Carew ([William Smith](/wiki/William_Smith_(actor) "William Smith (actor)")), Lori Wilson ([Sharon Farrell](/wiki/Sharon_Farrell "Sharon Farrell")), and Truck ([Moe Keale](/wiki/Moe_Keale "Moe Keale")) were introduced in season 12 alongside returning regular character Duke Lukela.[[2]](#cite_note-Terrace-3)

Most episodes of *Hawaii Five-O* ended with the arrest of criminals and McGarrett snapping, "Book 'em." The offense occasionally was added after this phrase, for example, "Book 'em, murder one." In many episodes, this was directed to Danny "Danno" Williams and became McGarrett's [catchphrase](/wiki/Catchphrase "Catchphrase"): "Book 'em, Danno."[[5]](#cite_note-Newcomb-6) This catchphrase also expanded to sports in the mid-1970s with former [Pittsburgh Penguins](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Penguins "Pittsburgh Penguins") announcer [Mike Lange](/wiki/Mike_Lange "Mike Lange"), who would utter the line for some Penguins goals.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Jack_Lord_Hawaii_Five-O.jpg/250px-Jack_Lord_Hawaii_Five-O.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jack_Lord_Hawaii_Five-O.jpg)

[Jack Lord](/wiki/Jack_Lord "Jack Lord") played Steve McGarrett.

McGarrett's tousled yet immaculate hairstyle, as well as his proclivity for wearing a dark suit and tie on all possible occasions (uncommon in the islands), rapidly entered popular culture. While the other members of *Five-O* "dressed mainland" much of the time, they also often wore local styles, such as the ubiquitous [aloha shirt](/wiki/Aloha_shirt "Aloha shirt").

In many episodes (including the pilot), McGarrett is drawn into the world of international [espionage](/wiki/Espionage "Espionage") and [national intelligence](/wiki/Military_espionage "Military espionage"). McGarrett's nemesis is a rogue intelligence officer of the [People's Republic of China](/wiki/People%27s_Republic_of_China "People's Republic of China") named Wo Fat. The [communist](/wiki/Communist "Communist") rogue agent was played by veteran actor [Khigh Dhiegh](/wiki/Khigh_Dhiegh "Khigh Dhiegh"). In the show's final episode in 1980, titled "Woe to Wo Fat", McGarrett finally sees his foe go to jail.[[4]](#cite_note-Snauffer-5)

Unlike the reboot, the show's action and straightforward storytelling left little time for personal stories involving wives or girlfriends,[[5]](#cite_note-Newcomb-6) though a two-part story in the first season dealt with the loss of McGarrett's sister's baby. Occasionally, a show would flash back to McGarrett's younger years or to a romantic figure.

In the episode "Number One with a Bullet, Part 2", McGarrett tells a criminal, "It was a bastard like you who killed my father." His 42-year-old father had been run down and killed by someone who had just held up a supermarket. Because Steve McGarrett is also a [commander](/wiki/Commander_(United_States) "Commander (United States)") in the [Naval Reserve](/wiki/United_States_Navy_Reserve "United States Navy Reserve"), he sometimes uses their resources to help investigate and solve crimes. The United States Navy as well as the U.S. Coast Guard provided some locations and resources for filming, hence the closing credits of some episodes acknowledge the assistance of the United States Navy.[[6]](#cite_note-7) A 1975 episode involving Danno's aunt, played by MacArthur's mother [Helen Hayes](/wiki/Helen_Hayes "Helen Hayes"), provided a bit of Williams' back story.

## Creation of the show

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hawaii_Five-O_(1968_TV_series)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Creation of the show")]

Sources differ on how the show came to be. Producer Leonard Freeman moved to Hawaiʻi to recuperate after suffering a heart attack. One source states the idea for the show may have come from a conversation Freeman had with Hawaiʻi's then-Governor [John A. Burns](/wiki/John_A._Burns "John A. Burns").[[7]](#cite_note-fiveofaq-8)[[8]](#cite_note-9)

Another source instead claims that Freeman wanted to set a show in [San Pedro, Los Angeles, California](/wiki/San_Pedro,_Los_Angeles,_California "San Pedro, Los Angeles, California") until his friend [Richard Boone](/wiki/Richard_Boone "Richard Boone") convinced him to shoot it entirely in Hawaiʻi.[[9]](#cite_note-boonerichar-10) A third source claims Freeman discussed the show with Governor Burns only after pitching the idea to CBS.[[10]](#cite_note-tvgjan69-11) Before settling on the name "Hawaii Five-O", Freeman considered titling the show "The Man".[[7]](#cite_note-fiveofaq-8)

### Casting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hawaii_Five-O_(1968_TV_series)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Casting")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/3/3c/Chin_Ho_head_shot.jpg/250px-Chin_Ho_head_shot.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chin_Ho_head_shot.jpg)

[Kam Fong Chun](/wiki/Kam_Fong_Chun "Kam Fong Chun") played Chin Ho Kelly.

Freeman offered [Richard Boone](/wiki/Richard_Boone "Richard Boone") the part of McGarrett, but Boone turned it down;[[9]](#cite_note-boonerichar-10) [Gregory Peck](/wiki/Gregory_Peck "Gregory Peck")[[10]](#cite_note-tvgjan69-11) and [Robert Brown](/wiki/Robert_Brown_(US_actor) "Robert Brown (US actor)")[[11]](#cite_note-12) were also considered. Ultimately, [Jack Lord](/wiki/Jack_Lord "Jack Lord") – then living in Beverly Hills – was asked at the last moment. Lord read for the part on a Wednesday, was cast, and flew to Hawaiʻi two days later. On the following Monday, Lord was in front of the cameras. Freeman and Lord had worked together previously on an unsold TV pilot called *Grand Hotel*.[[10]](#cite_note-tvgjan69-11)

[Tim O'Kelly](/wiki/Tim_O%27Kelly "Tim O'Kelly") originated the role of [Danny "Danno" Williams](/wiki/Danny_%22Danno%22_Williams "Danny \"Danno\" Williams") in the pilot episode, "Cocoon". Test audiences apparently were not positive on O'Kelly, however, and the producers replaced him with [James MacArthur](/wiki/James_MacArthur "James MacArthur").

[Kam Fong Chun](/wiki/Kam_Fong_Chun "Kam Fong Chun"), an 18-year veteran of the [Honolulu Police Department](/wiki/Honolulu_Police_Department "Honolulu Police Department"), auditioned for the part of the lead villain Wo Fat, but Freeman cast him in the part of Chin Ho Kelly instead. Freeman took the name Wo Fat from a restaurant in downtown Honolulu. The name Chin Ho came from [Chinn Ho](/wiki/Chinn_Ho "Chinn Ho"), the owner of the [Ilikai Hotel](/wiki/The_Ilikai "The Ilikai") where the penthouse shot of Steve McGarrett in the opening title sequence was taken.[[12]](#cite_note-Gomes-13) [Richard Denning](/wiki/Richard_Denning "Richard Denning"), who played the governor, had retired to Hawaiʻi and came out of retirement for the show. Zulu was a Waikīkī beach boy and local DJ with no acting experience when he was cast for the part of Kono, which he played for the next four years.

## Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hawaii_Five-O_(1968_TV_series)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Production")]

The first season was shot in a rusty military [Quonset hut](/wiki/Quonset_hut "Quonset hut") in [Pearl City](/wiki/Pearl_City,_Hawaii "Pearl City, Hawaii"), which the various cast members quickly nicknamed "Mongoose Manor".[[13]](#cite_note-14) The roof tended to leak, and rats would often gnaw at the cables. The show then moved to a [Fort Ruger](/wiki/Fort_Ruger "Fort Ruger") location for seasons two to eight. A third studio was built at [Diamond Head](/wiki/Diamond_Head,_Hawaii "Diamond Head, Hawaii"), and was used during the last four seasons.

A problem from the beginning was the lack of a movie industry in Hawaiʻi. Much of the crew and cast, including many locals who ended up participating in the show, had to learn their respective jobs as they went along. Jack Lord was known as a perfectionist who insisted on the best from everyone.[[14]](#cite_note-obit-15) His temper flared when he felt that others did not give their best, but in later reunions they admitted that Lord's hard-driving force had made them better actors and made *Hawaii Five-O* a better show. Lord's high standards helped the show last another six years after Leonard Freeman's death from heart trouble during the sixth season.[[14]](#cite_note-obit-15)

Lord said the show discarded more scripts than any show on television. He would not tolerate or shoot any scripts that he deemed subpar.[[15]](#cite_note-16)

Most episodes had McGarrett leading the investigations but occasionally, episodes would focus on the other actors, and let them showcase their own talents, such as Danno defusing bombs in "The Clock Struck Twelve".[[16]](#cite_note-17)

Very few episodes were shot outside of Hawaiʻi. At least two episodes were shot on location in Los Angeles, one in Hong Kong, and one in Singapore as McGarrett traveled to those locales for Investigations.

Each episode was shot in seven days, and in 1975 the cost of shooting each episode was $290,000. At the time it was the most expensive show on the air.[[17]](#cite_note-Newspapers.com-18)

## Credits

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hawaii_Five-O_(1968_TV_series)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Credits")]

The opening title sequence was created by television director [Reza S. Badiyi](/wiki/Reza_Badiyi "Reza Badiyi"). Early shows began with a [cold open](/wiki/Cold_open "Cold open") suggesting the sinister plot for that episode, then cut to a shot of a big ocean wave and the start of the theme song.[[5]](#cite_note-Newcomb-6)[[18]](#cite_note-originalcredits-19)[[19]](#cite_note-20) A fast zoom-in to the top balcony of the [Ilikai Hotel](/wiki/Ilikai_Hotel_%26_Luxury_Suites "Ilikai Hotel & Luxury Suites") followed,[[12]](#cite_note-Gomes-13) showing McGarrett turning to face the camera, followed by many quick-cuts and freeze-frames of Hawaiian scenery, including [Punchbowl Cemetery](/wiki/National_Memorial_Cemetery_of_the_Pacific "National Memorial Cemetery of the Pacific"), and Hawaiian-Chinese-English model[[20]](#cite_note-21) Elizabeth Malamalamaokalani Logue turning to face the camera.[[21]](#cite_note-22) A grass-skirted [hula](/wiki/Hula "Hula") dancer from the pilot episode was also included, played by Helen Kuoha-Torco, who later became a business professor at [Windward Community College](/wiki/Windward_Community_College "Windward Community College").[[22]](#cite_note-23) The opening scene ended with shots of the supporting players, and the flashing blue light of a police motorcycle racing through a Honolulu street.

At the conclusion of each episode, Jack Lord narrated a promo for the next episode, often emphasizing the "guest villain", especially if the villain is a recurring character, such as that played by actor [Hume Cronyn](/wiki/Hume_Cronyn "Hume Cronyn") (2 episodes). The line he spoke was, "This is Jack Lord inviting you to be with us next week for <name of episode>" and then, "Be here. Aloha." The promos were removed from the syndicated episodes but most have been restored in DVD releases from the second season through the ninth. Most of the promos are slightly edited to remove references to "next week".

This tradition has been continued in the 2010 version of *[Hawaii Five-0](/wiki/Hawaii_Five-0_(2010_TV_series) "Hawaii Five-0 (2010 TV series)")*, but is not limited to [Alex O'Loughlin](/wiki/Alex_O%27Loughlin "Alex O'Loughlin"). All of the primary cast members take turns with the "Be here. Aloha." line at the end of the preview segment.

There were two versions of the closing credits portion of the show. During the first season, the theme music was played over a short film of a flashing blue light attached to the rear of a police motorcycle in [Waikīkī](/wiki/Waikiki "Waikiki") heading west (the film is shown at twice the normal speed, as can be seen from people crossing a street behind the police motorcycle).[[23]](#cite_note-24) In later seasons, the same music was played over film of outrigger canoeists battling the surf.

In a 2010 issue of *[TV Guide](/wiki/TV_Guide "TV Guide")*, the show's opening title sequence ranked No. 4 on a list of TV's top 10 credits sequences, as selected by readers.[[24]](#cite_note-25)

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hawaii_Five-O_(1968_TV_series)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Legacy")]

The show was the longest-running police procedural show on American television until *[Law & Order](/wiki/Law_%26_Order "Law & Order")* surpassed it in 2002, and was the first to enjoy an uninterrupted run that exceeded a decade (it has since been joined in that distinction by several other series including *[Law & Order: Special Victims Unit](/wiki/Law_%26_Order:_Special_Victims_Unit "Law & Order: Special Victims Unit")*, *[CSI: Crime Scene Investigation](/wiki/CSI:_Crime_Scene_Investigation "CSI: Crime Scene Investigation")* and [*NCIS*](/wiki/NCIS_(TV_series) "NCIS (TV series)")).

When the show premiered in 1968, Hawaiʻi had been a state for only nine years and was relatively obscure to Americans who had never served in the [Pacific Theater](/wiki/Pacific_War "Pacific War"), but as a geographic part of [Polynesia](/wiki/Polynesia "Polynesia") it had an exotic image.

Known for the location, theme song, and ensemble cast, *Hawaii Five-O* contains a heavy use of exterior location shooting throughout the entire 12 seasons. A typical episode, on average, would have at least two-thirds of all footage shot on location, as opposed to a "typical" show of the time which would be shot largely on [sound stages](/wiki/Sound_stage "Sound stage") and [backlots](/wiki/Backlot "Backlot"). It is also remembered for its unusual setting during a time when most crime dramas of the era were set in or around the [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles") or [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") areas.

The Hawaii-based television show *[Magnum, P.I.](/wiki/Magnum,_P.I. "Magnum, P.I.")* was created after *Hawaii Five-O* ended its run, in order to make further use of the expensive production facilities created there for *Five-O*. The first few *Magnum P.I.* episodes made direct references to *Five-O*, suggesting that it takes place in the same [fictional setting](/wiki/Fictional_setting "Fictional setting"). In Episode 10 of Season 2, "The Taking of Dick McWilliams," Rick Wright answers Thomas Magnum when asked about a license plate search that the car belonged to someone on Five-O named McGarrett. *Magnum*'s producers made a few attempts to coax Jack Lord out of retirement for a [cameo appearance](/wiki/Cameo_appearance "Cameo appearance"), but he refused.[[4]](#cite_note-Snauffer-5)

Many local people were cast in the show, which was ethnically diverse by the standards of the late 1960s.[[5]](#cite_note-Newcomb-6) The first run and syndication were seen by an estimated 400 million people around the world.

"Bored, She Hung Herself", the 16th episode of the second season, depicted a Five-O investigation into the apparent suicide of a woman by hanging, which she was supposedly practicing as part of a health regimen.[[25]](#cite_note-26) A viewer reportedly died trying the same technique, and as a result, the episode was not rebroadcast, was never included in any syndication packages, and has not been included on any DVD release of the show to date.[[26]](#cite_note-27)[[27]](#cite_note-28) The family of the person who died in the real-life hanging sued CBS over the episode.[[28]](#cite_note-29)

### Remake projects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hawaii_Five-O_(1968_TV_series)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Remake projects")]

#### 1990s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hawaii_Five-O_(1968_TV_series)&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 1990s")]

In July 1996, it was announced producer [George Litto](/wiki/George_Litto "George Litto") had partnered with [Leonard Freeman's](/wiki/Leonard_Freeman "Leonard Freeman") estate and his widow, Rose, in developing a theatrical adaptation of *Hawaii Five-O* with a $50 million-$70 million budget and franchise potential.[[29]](#cite_note-Hawaii5OMovie-30) For the lead role of [Steve McGarrett](/wiki/Steve_McGarrett "Steve McGarrett"), Litto expressed a desire for [Mel Gibson](/wiki/Mel_Gibson "Mel Gibson"), [Michael Douglas](/wiki/Michael_Douglas "Michael Douglas") or [Harrison Ford](/wiki/Harrison_Ford "Harrison Ford") while former McGarrett actor [Jack Lord](/wiki/Jack_Lord "Jack Lord") would play a Governor or Senator from Hawaii.[[29]](#cite_note-Hawaii5OMovie-30) Directors whom Litto expressed interest in helming the project included [Andrew Davis](/wiki/Andrew_Davis_(director) "Andrew Davis (director)"), [Brian De Palma](/wiki/Brian_De_Palma "Brian De Palma"), and [Paul Verhoeven](/wiki/Paul_Verhoeven "Paul Verhoeven").[[29]](#cite_note-Hawaii5OMovie-30) Development on a feature film was stonewalled when [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS") filed suit against the Freeman estate claiming ownership and usage of *Hawaii Five-O* rested with them and not the Freemans.[[30]](#cite_note-CBSLawsuit-31) The suit was eventually settled in favor of Freeman and Litto.[[31]](#cite_note-32)

A one-hour pilot for a new series was made in 1996 but never aired. Produced and written by [Stephen J. Cannell](/wiki/Stephen_J._Cannell "Stephen J. Cannell"), it starred [Gary Busey](/wiki/Gary_Busey "Gary Busey") and [Russell Wong](/wiki/Russell_Wong "Russell Wong") as the new Five-O team. James MacArthur returned as Dan Williams, having become governor of Hawaii.[[32]](#cite_note-33) Several cameos were made by other *Five-O* regulars, including Kam Fong as Chin Ho Kelly[[33]](#cite_note-34) (even though the character had been killed off at the end of Season 10).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### DreamWorks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hawaii_Five-O_(1968_TV_series)&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: DreamWorks")]

In April 2002, Another attempt was made by Litto to turn *Hawaii Five-O* into a feature film with a [film package](/wiki/Movie_packaging "Movie packaging") he put together, that included a script by Roger Towne and an expected budget of $100 million, becoming the subject of a bidding war among several studios until [DreamWorks Pictures](/wiki/DreamWorks_Pictures "DreamWorks Pictures") emerged with exclusive negotiating rights.[[34]](#cite_note-HawaiiFiveODW3-35)[[35]](#cite_note-HawaiiFiveODW-36) Later that same month however, Dreamworks lost their position as exclusive negotiator after the studio was unwilling to meet Litto's condition that would have given him and his heirs [in-perpetuity](/wiki/Perpetuity "Perpetuity") [creative control](/wiki/Artistic_control "Artistic control") over *Hawaii Five-O* in a manner similar to the [Broccoli family's](/wiki/Albert_R._Broccoli "Albert R. Broccoli") control over the [*James Bond* films](/wiki/Portrayal_of_James_Bond_in_film "Portrayal of James Bond in film").[[36]](#cite_note-HawaiiFiveODW2-37) Litto expressed no regret at the deal with DreamWorks collapsing and stated:

> ...this was more about the reluctance to give up rights if the studio put up all the money. I've been involved in the series since its inception and have never rushed to remake this. What mattered to me was this: What is the script? Who plays McGarrett? And who directs? I wouldn’t waive my rights to be part of those decisions, because I believe if the first is done right, it has the franchise potential of James Bond or Mission: Impossible. Do it wrong the first time and it's over.[[34]](#cite_note-HawaiiFiveODW3-35)

#### *Hawaii Five-0*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hawaii_Five-O_(1968_TV_series)&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Hawaii Five-0")]

A remake pilot, called *[Hawaii Five-0](/wiki/Hawaii_Five-0_(2010_TV_series) "Hawaii Five-0 (2010 TV series)")* (the last character is a zero instead of the letter "O"; the original series used an "O" as zero was typed as a capital O), aired September 20, 2010, on [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS"). It lasted for 10 seasons until the 240th and final episode was aired on April 3, 2020. The remake version *Hawaii Five-0* used the same principal character names as the original, and the new Steve McGarrett's late father's vintage 1974 [Mercury Marquis](/wiki/Mercury_Marquis "Mercury Marquis") was the actual car driven by Lord in the original series' final seasons.

The new series opening credit sequence was an homage to the original; the theme song was cut in half, from 60 to 30 seconds, but was an otherwise identical instrumentation. Most of the iconic shots were replicated, beginning with the helicopter approach and close-up turn of McGarrett at the [Ilikai Hotel](/wiki/Ilikai_Hotel_%26_Luxury_Suites "Ilikai Hotel & Luxury Suites") penthouse, the jet engine [nacelle](/wiki/Nacelle "Nacelle"), a hula dancer's hips, the quickly stepped zoom-in to the face of the [Lady Columbia statue](/wiki/Columbia_(name)#Personification "Columbia (name)") at [Punchbowl](/wiki/National_Memorial_Cemetery_of_the_Pacific "National Memorial Cemetery of the Pacific"), the close-up of the [Kamehameha Statue](/wiki/Kamehameha_Statue "Kamehameha Statue")'s face, and the ending with a police motorcycle's [flashing blue light](/wiki/Emergency_vehicle_lighting "Emergency vehicle lighting").

The surname of recurring character Governor Sam Denning (played by [Richard T. Jones](/wiki/Richard_T._Jones "Richard T. Jones")) was a nod to actor [Richard Denning](/wiki/Richard_Denning "Richard Denning"), who played the Governor in the original series. Starting with Season 7, many of the clips that were part of the original opening were removed and more action shots of the cast were included. On the March 19, 2012 episode, [Ed Asner](/wiki/Ed_Asner "Ed Asner") reprised his role as "August March", a character he first played in a 1975 episode. Clips from the 1975 episode were included in the new one, even though the 2010 series was intended to be in a different narrative universe than the Jack Lord series. The 2016 episode "Makaukau ʻoe e Paʻani?" features a sequence in which McGarrett (played by [Alex O'Loughlin](/wiki/Alex_O%27Loughlin "Alex O'Loughlin")) briefly interacts with a CGI reconstruction of [Jack Lord](/wiki/Jack_Lord "Jack Lord").

### Theme music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hawaii_Five-O_(1968_TV_series)&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Theme music")]

Main article: [Hawaii Five-O Theme](/wiki/Hawaii_Five-O_Theme "Hawaii Five-O Theme")

Another legacy of the show is the popularity of the *Hawaii Five-O* theme music. The tune was composed by [Morton Stevens](/wiki/Morton_Stevens "Morton Stevens"), who also composed numerous episode scores performed by the CBS Orchestra. The theme was later recorded by [the Ventures](/wiki/The_Ventures "The Ventures"), whose version reached No. 4 on the [*Billboard* Hot 100](/wiki/Billboard_Hot_100 "Billboard Hot 100") pop chart,[[5]](#cite_note-Newcomb-6) and is particularly popular with college and high school [marching bands](/wiki/Marching_band "Marching band"), especially at the [University of Hawaiʻi](/wiki/University_of_Hawai%CA%BBi_at_M%C4%81noa "University of Hawaiʻi at Mānoa") where it has become the unofficial fight song. Fans mimic swimming during the playing of the song.

The tune has also been heard at [Robertson Stadium](/wiki/Robertson_Stadium "Robertson Stadium") after [Houston Dynamo](/wiki/Houston_Dynamo "Houston Dynamo") goals scored by [Brian Ching](/wiki/Brian_Ching "Brian Ching"), a native of Hawaii. Because of the tempo of the music, the theme gained popularity in the UK with followers of [Northern soul](/wiki/Northern_soul "Northern soul") and was popular on [dance floors](/wiki/Performance_surface "Performance surface") in the 1970s.[[37]](#cite_note-Northern_Soul-38)

### Slang term for police

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hawaii_Five-O_(1968_TV_series)&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Slang term for police")]

The phrase "five-O" (or any variation, such as "5–0", "5-O", and "five O") [/ˈfaɪv oʊ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*FYVE oh*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) has often come to refer to the [police](/wiki/Police "Police") in the United States.[[38]](#cite_note-Rhodes1997-39)

### Syndication

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hawaii_Five-O_(1968_TV_series)&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Syndication")]

*Hawaii Five-O* survived long enough to overlap with reruns of early episodes, which were broadcast by CBS in their late night schedule while new episodes were still being produced. Once the program entered [syndication](/wiki/Broadcast_syndication "Broadcast syndication") after the original run of the series, CBS broadcast reruns of season 12 in late night under the title *McGarrett* to avoid confusion with the episodes in syndication broadcast under the title *Hawaii Five-O.*
In the United Kingdom, the series first aired on [ITV](/wiki/ITV_network "ITV network") on July 19, 1970, in a Saturday evening time slot.

In Hawaii, reruns of the series aired on [13 Alanui](/wiki/13th_Street_(TV_channel) "13th Street (TV channel)") beginning on August 1, 2001 (the day [13 Alanui](/wiki/13th_Street_(TV_channel) "13th Street (TV channel)") began broadcasting) and continued until December 30, 2011.

From September 2021 to mid October 2023, the series aired in Ontario, Canada intermittently weekdays either at 1pm or 2pm on CHCH TV 11. CHCH did air the HD remastered version of the series in its original unedited broadcast versions. CHCH did air all 12 seasons in broadcast order.

Since 2023, the first ten seasons stream three episodes per day on Pluto TV in Canada. Episodes are also remastered in HD and uncut.

Beginning in January of 2022, [Heroes & Icons](/wiki/Heroes_%26_Icons "Heroes & Icons") picked up the show, and it ran until December of 2025. As of 2026, it continues to air on H&I's sister networks, [MeTV](/wiki/MeTV "MeTV") and MeTV+.[[40]](#cite_note-42)

In September 2019 (Region 4, Australia), Via Vision Entertainment released a *Season 1-7 Boxset* followed by *Season 8-12 Boxset* in February 2020. The Via Vision Entertainment releases are only available in these box sets and not individual seasons.

The episode "Bored, She Hung Herself" is not included in *The Second Season* set. The omission is mentioned on the back of the box. Only some Australian bootlegs have had the episode. Seasons 2–8 contain episode promos by [Jack Lord](/wiki/Jack_Lord "Jack Lord").

On December 3, 2013, Paramount released *Hawaii Five-O – The Complete Series* on DVD in Region 1.[[44]](#cite_note-46) On April 18, 2017, *The Complete Series* set was reissued.

## Other media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hawaii_Five-O_(1968_TV_series)&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Other media")]

A [soundtrack album](/wiki/Soundtrack_album "Soundtrack album") featuring [Morton Stevens](/wiki/Morton_Stevens "Morton Stevens")' theme and incidental music from the pilot and the first two seasons was issued by [Capitol Records](/wiki/Capitol_Records "Capitol Records") in 1970. Unlike many albums of television music of the time, the music was taken directly from the scoring sessions rather than being specially re-recorded for album release. One of the instrumental pieces on the album, "Call to Danger", was originally recorded for the unsold 1967 pilot of the same name and also excerpted as background music accompanying a "Special Presentation" logo that [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS") used to introduce its prime time [television specials](/wiki/Television_special "Television special") throughout the 1970s and 1980s. The album was re-issued on compact disc by [Film Score Monthly](/wiki/Film_Score_Monthly "Film Score Monthly") in 2010.

*Hawaii Five-O* was the subject of six original novels. The first two books were published by Signet Paperbacks in 1968 and 1969. After that were two juvenile hard covers published by Whitman publishing in 1969 and 1971 and finally two more books were published in England.[[46]](#cite_note-48)
